items.md: ## 0. FOREWORD
items.value: 0. FOREWORD

items.md: **0.1** This Indian Standard was adopted by the Indian Standards Institution on 3 September 1968, after the draft finalized by the Acoustics Sectional Committee had been approved by the Electrotechnical Division Council.
items.value: 0.1 This Indian Standard was adopted by the Indian Standards Institution on 3 September 1968, after the draft finalized by the Acoustics Sectional Committee had been approved by the Electrotechnical Division Council.

items.md: **0.2** This standard concerns with the procedures followed in the objective measurements of noise emitted by machines. These procedures are not necessarily applicable to noise of an impulsive character.
items.value: 0.2 This standard concerns with the procedures followed in the objective measurements of noise emitted by machines. These procedures are not necessarily applicable to noise of an impulsive character.

items.md: **0.3** This standard describes the general principles of methods of measurements of machinery noise based on which individual standards may be formulated. The specific standard covering methods of measurement of noise of the various types of machines will have to select the most suitable method having regard to the size of the machine and its application. All the necessary particulars to enable a result to be obtained with the required accuracy will be given in these standards.
items.value: 0.3 This standard describes the general principles of methods of measurements of machinery noise based on which individual standards may be formulated. The specific standard covering methods of measurement of noise of the various types of machines will have to select the most suitable method having regard to the size of the machine and its application. All the necessary particulars to enable a result to be obtained with the required accuracy will be given in these standards.

items.md: **0.4** The measurement of the physical characteristics of the noise of machines has the following four objects:
items.value: 0.4 The measurement of the physical characteristics of the noise of machines has the following four objects:

items.md:
* a) To verify that the noise of a given machine conforms to a certain standard,
* b) To make a comparison between the noise emitted by machines built to the same specification,
* c) To make a comparison between the noise emitted by different machines, and
* d) To determine the noise received at a distance.
a) To verify that the noise of a given machine conforms to a certain standard,
items.value: a) To verify that the noise of a given machine conforms to a certain standard,

items.md: b) To make a comparison between the noise emitted by machines built to the same specification,
items.value: b) To make a comparison between the noise emitted by machines built to the same specification,

items.md: c) To make a comparison between the noise emitted by different machines, and
items.value: c) To make a comparison between the noise emitted by different machines, and

items.md: d) To determine the noise received at a distance.
items.value: d) To determine the noise received at a distance.

items.md: The four aims specified above are achieved by determining the acoustic noise power radiated by the machine as well as the directivity, these quantities being measured as a function of the frequency. Determination of the acoustic power should thus be the preferred method for the measurement of the noise of machines. The different methods of measuring acoustic power are given in Appendices A to C.
items.value: The four aims specified above are achieved by determining the acoustic noise power radiated by the machine as well as the directivity, these quantities being measured as a function of the frequency. Determination of the acoustic power should thus be the preferred method for the measurement of the noise of machines. items.md: **0.4.1** Determination of the power and the directivity, however, leads to the siting of the points of measurement sufficiently removed from the machine for the conditions of propagation by progressive waves to be established. It is also necessary that at this site, the reflections from the walls of the premises shall have no effect. This requires premises of relatively large dimensions compared with those of the machine, with very absorbent walls.

**0.4.2** It is sometimes possible to use rooms with very reverberant walls, where the acoustics field tends to become uniform. But the directivity of the noise radiated cannot be determined under these conditions. In addition, the problems of the special premises remain the same.
items.value: 0.4.2 It is sometimes possible to use rooms with very reverberant walls, where the acoustics field tends to become uniform. But the directivity of the noise radiated cannot be determined under these conditions. In addition, the problems of the special premises remain the same.

items.md: **0.4.3** It may also be possible to use semi-reverberant spaces but this method can provide only the limited information on the directivity of acoustic radiation and very rough estimation of the acoustic power.
items.value: 0.4.3 It may also be possible to use semi-reverberant spaces but this method can provide only the limited information on the directivity of acoustic radiation and very rough estimation of the acoustic power.

items.md: **0.4.4** While there will be no difficulty to provide necessary acoustic environment for small machines, it may not always be possible to achieve the necessary conditions for large machines, special installation condition of the machine may be required. These conditions cannot always be met particularly for large machines. It may not also be possible to bring the machine to a room suitable for the determination of the acoustic power. Hence, use is often made of a simplified method in which sound pressure measurements are carried out at certain measurement points suitably spread around the outer casing of the machine, sufficiently close to the machine to obviate the effect of the sound reflections. Such a method enables the object (a) to be achieved with the desired precision, the dimensions of the machine being generally sufficiently well-defined, but it does not enable objects (c) and (d) to be obtained. It is, however, possible for the acoustic conditions at a distance to be evaluated approximately on the basis of pressure measurements carried out in the vicinity of the machine by taking into account the surface on which the measurements have been carried out.

It is, however, possible for the acoustic conditions at a distance to be evaluated approximately on the basis of pressure measurements carried out in the vicinity of the machine by taking into account the surface on which the measurements have been carried out.

items.md: # 4. METHODS OF MEASUREMENT
items.value: 4. METHODS OF MEASUREMENT

items.md: **4.1** The accurate determination of the acoustic power of a machine ( *see* **0.4** ) has to be performed either as a far-field sound pressure level measurement under hemispherical radiation or free-field conditions, or as a sound pressure level measurement in a reverberant room under diffuse field conditions. In cases where it is difficult to take sound pressure level measurements under these conditions, the measurements may have to be performed under semi-reverberant conditions ( *see* Appendix C), or near-field measurements ( *see* Appendix D ) be made.
items.value: 4.1 The accurate determination of the acoustic power of a machine ( see 0.4 ) has to be performed either as a far-field sound pressure level measurement under hemispherical radiation or free-field conditions, or as a sound pressure level measurement in a reverberant room under diffuse field conditions. In cases where it is difficult to take sound pressure level measurements under these conditions, the measurements may have to be performed under semi-reverberant conditions ( see Appendix C), or near-field measurements ( see Appendix D ) be made.

items.md: **4.1.1** The following four methods are available for the determination of acoustic noise power of the machine and/or the sound pressure level of the noise emitted:
items.value: 4.1.1 The following four methods are available for the determination of acoustic noise power of the machine and/or the sound pressure level of the noise emitted:

items.md: a) *Hemispherical radiation and free-field methods* — The basic principle of this method and evaluation of the radiated acoustic noise power and its directivity are described in Appendix A;
items.value: a) Hemispherical radiation and free-field methods — The basic principle of this method and evaluation of the radiated acoustic noise power and its directivity are described in Appendix A;

items.md: b) *Diffuse-field method* — The basic principle of this method and evaluation of acoustic noise power are described in Appendix B. This method does not provide information on the directivity of the acoustic radiation. Since the degree of diffusion is dependent upon the nature of the noise source, this method is not recommended for machines the noise from which contains prominent components;

items.md: ## 6. CHOICE OF METHOD
items.value: 6. CHOICE OF METHOD

items.md: **6.1** The objects specified in **0.4** are achieved by determining the acoustic power radiated by the machine as well as the directivity. Hence the method that permits the determination of acoustic power is recommended. However, the choice of method depends on the size of the machine and its application.

items.md: ### A-1. PRINCIPLE OF MEASUREMENT
items.value: A-1. PRINCIPLE OF MEASUREMENT

items.md: **A-1.1** The determination of the power and the directivity requires the siting of measurements points sufficiently remote from the machine for the conditions of propagation of progressive sound waves to be established. There should be no reflected waves at the measurement points and this requires premises with sufficiently absorbent walls and dimensions large compared with those of machine. Any effect of the surroundings or near-field effects will be made apparent by measuring over two surfaces and checking if the sound pressure varies inversely as the square root of the ratio of surface areas. A ratio of areas of 2 or 4 is recommended, corresponding to $-3$ and $-6$ dB respectively. The results of such check measurements should be stated.

items.md: The quantity $p^2\_m$ is the mean value in space of the square of the rms sound pressures recorded at a certain number of measuring points, on a test hemisphere of radius $r$ with the machine as its centre. It is necessary that $r$ be sufficiently large for the measuring points to come within the field of progressive waves where sound pressure and particle velocity are in phase. In the most usual cases, a value of $r$ of the order of twice the dimensions of the machine suffices to fulfil this condition. In the above expression the acoustic impedance of the air is assumed to be equal to $\rho C = 400 \text{ mks (rayls)}$. When the machine is not mounted on a reflecting plane but is in free space, the radiation becomes spherical and not hemispherical and $2\pi r^2$ should be replaced by $4\pi r^2$.

items.md: ## DETERMINATION OF SOUND POWER UNDER SEMI-REVERBERANT CONDITIONS
items.value: DETERMINATION OF SOUND POWER UNDER SEMI-REVERBERANT CONDITIONS

items.md: ### C-1. PRINCIPLE OF MEASUREMENT
items.value: C-1. PRINCIPLE OF MEASUREMENT

items.md: **C-1.1** In practice, it is often necessary for machines to be tested in room having limited absorption (semi-reverberant rooms), such as factories, shops, offices. Under these conditions, the sound power of the machine under test can be determined by using a reference sound source of known acoustic power $P\_r$ which is substituted for the machine. If it is not possible for the machine under test to be moved, and if the test room is large enough, another suitable position for the reference source can usually be found. The reference sound source can be a machine of similar type or a special sound source radiating noise having a sufficiently uniform spectral distribution over the whole frequency range of interest. Determination of the sound power of the reference source is to be carried out by the method described in Appendix A and Appendix B, or the result can also be provided by a testing laboratory.

items.md: **C-2.2** In many cases a region of the sound field can be found where the sound pressure level beyond the distance $r$ does not vary appreciably with position. In that event, the above equation can still be used to calculate $P$. The quantities $p^2\_m$ and $p^2\_{mr}$ then represent the mean values of the squares of the rms sound pressures recorded at a certain number of points in the reverberant field of the test room. This procedure can also be followed in a reverberant room (*see* Appendix B).

items.md: ## D-2. CALCULATION OF THE SOUND PRESSURE LEVEL AT THE REFERENCE RADIUS
items.value: D-2. CALCULATION OF THE SOUND PRESSURE LEVEL AT THE REFERENCE RADIUS

items.md: **D-2.1** The sound pressure level at a reference radius which is laid down in the specific individual standards is calculated from that measured on the prescribed surface as described in **D-2.2**. The reference radius is to be laid down in each individual standard. It should be the same of all machines covered by a given standard. One of the values 1, 3 or 10 m should be adopted. It is recommended that the value chosen should be slightly greater than the radius of the equivalent hemisphere corresponding to the largest machine covered by the individual standard.
